title: Clean Fuels Weekly Commentary: CA LCFS Volumes Rise and Spot Prices and Average Type 1 Prices Witness a Decline
content:
This Weekâ€™s Highlights:

(24th February â€“ 02nd March)
  • A total of 1,125,578 credits were exchanged as Type 1 trades, a rise of 65.00% week-over-week (WoW), and all non-zero trades witnessed a rise of 77.90% WoW as 1,695,578 credits were traded in CA LCFS.
  • The weekly average CA LCFS credit price decreased this week to $70.42/MTÂ for Type 1Â transfers, recording a 0.98%Â fall WoW; All Non-Zero transfers rose by 0.24% in price, reaching $70.14/MT.
  • The average LCFS spot credit price decreased by 8.11% WoW; on 04th March, the spot price stood at $54.75/MT and the benchmark price stood at $58.00/MT.
  • D3 RIN prices increased by 0.40% WoW whereas D4, D5 and D6 RIN prices decreased by 11.49% WoW each.

cCarbonâ€™s Market View

Type 1 average prices and spot prices have been consistently declining, but this week saw a rise in trading volumes. Despite the ongoing downward trend in prices, the market experienced increased activity, reflecting a shift in trading dynamics. The OAL (Office of Administrative Law) disapproval of the LCFS (Low Carbon Fuel Standard) amendments on February 18, 2025, caused a significant market shift. The rejection was based on clarity and procedural concerns, not the substance of the proposed changes, with 26 provisions identified as unclear. This led to a sharp decline in LCFS credit prices. However, CARB (California Air Resources Board) swiftly responded, emphasizing that the disapproval was technical and not a rejection of policy goals. CARB plans to revise the amendments, invite public comments on substantial changes, and resubmit them for approval. For more details, please read our article.

CA LCFS Weekly Price and Volume Trend

[caption id="attachment_593077" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average Type 1 prices recorded a 0.98% WoW fall, decreasing from $71.12 to $70.42 this week, while all non-zero credit prices increased by 0.24% WoW, rising from $69.97 to $70.14. Type 1 trades saw 1,125,578 credits exchanged, reflecting a rise of 65.00% WoW. The total transaction volume for all non-zero trades saw an increase of 77.90% to reach 1,695,578 credits.

CA LCFS Daily Average Spot Price

[caption id="attachment_593078" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 2. California LCFS Weekly average spot prices and benchmark (*CC.Info OTC: Numbers are an average of broker prices reported to CC.info)[/caption] Last week, the average LCFS spot credit prices decreased by 8.11% WoW, reaching $59.50/MT. On 04th March, the average spot price was $54.75/MT, while the benchmark, representing the future price of contracts expiring at the end of 2025, stood at $58.00/MT.

CA LCFS Weekly Trade Frequency and Volume

[caption id="attachment_593079" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 3. Trade frequency and volume, Source: California ARB[/caption] As per the data reported by CARB last week, 89 trades were reported in the market, with nine high-volume trades. The highest transfer by price was $76.57 for a Type 1 transaction at a volume of 650,000 credits, which was also the largest volume transfers. Last week, the lowest transfer by price was $47.00 per credit of Type 2, reported for a transaction at volume of 5,000 credits. The smallest volume transfers of 39 credits of the Type 1 category were undertaken for a price of $65.00.

CA LCFS Monthly Volumes and Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_593080" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 4. Total monthly volumes and associated prices, Source: California ARB[/caption] In February, after the OAL's disapproval of the LCFS amendments, the market experienced notable shifts. The average Type 1 spot price reached $72.15, with trading volumes for the month totaling 6.31 MMT. Compared to February 2024, when the average Type 1 spot price was $61.03, this marks an 18.22% increase year-over-year, with volumes standing at 1.96 MMT.

CA LCFS Weekly Volume Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_593081" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 5. Volume Price Trend indicator for the CA LCFS market, Source: California ARB[/caption] The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for a stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls. Across 24th Feb â€“ 02nd March, total VPT recorded a 0.96% increase (WoW), with all non-zero prices rising over the previous week.

Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS): RIN weekly price trend

RINs are credits used for compliance and are the â€œcurrencyâ€ of the RFS program. Renewable fuel producers generate RINs, market participants trade RINs, and obligated parties obtain and then ultimately retire RINs for the compliance list. D3 RIN prices increased by 0.40% WoW, D4, D5 and D6 RIN prices decreased by 11.49% WoW. D3: Cellulosic biofuel D4: Biomass-based diesel D5: Advanced biofuels (produced from any type of renewable biomass except corn starch ethanol) D6: Conventional renewable biofuel ethanol derived from corn starch, or any other qualifying renewable fuel

Upcoming dates

Key Clean Fuels Market Developments this week

  • Ahead of Summer Driving Season, EPA Allows Expanded E15 Access to Midwest States Year-Round.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) Administrator Lee Zeldin has announced the agencyâ€™s decision to uphold the current April 28, 2025, implementation date to provide parity for E15 and E10 in Midwestern states. This action, requested by the governors of Illinois, Iowa, Minnesota, Missouri, Nebraska, Ohio, South Dakota, and Wisconsin, provides regulatory certainty for fuel suppliers and expands consumer access to E15 year-round in these states.
  • Liiasâ€™ Bipartisan Bill Promoting Sustainable Aviation Fuel Advances to Senate Ways & Means Committee. Senate Bill 5601, sponsored by Sen. Marko Liias (D-Edmonds), incentivizes new sustainable fuels projects in the state like the SkyNRG project in Walla Walla and Paine Fieldâ€™s Sustainable Aviation Fuels Center. The bill would create a grant program to support SAF development and research in the state, and it would leverage certain tax deferrals and exemptions to create manufacturing jobs necessary for building the infrastructure for SAF innovation. The bipartisan bill to jumpstart Washingtonâ€™s sustainable aviation fuels (SAF) industry will be heard in the Senate Ways & Means Committee Feb. 25.
  • Air Products to Exit Three U.S.-Based Projects. As part of a review initiated by Air Productsâ€™ (NYSE:APD) newly-elected Board of Directors and Chief Executive Officer, the Company today announced its decision to exit three projects in the U.S. which includes Sustainable Aviation Fuel expansion project in Paramount, California, and green liquid hydrogen production in Massena, New York.
  • Oil Trade Group Pushes US for National Ethanol Policy After EPA Okays Midwest Expansion. The American Petroleum Institute trade group on Monday pressed for a nationwide policy on higher-ethanol blends of gasoline after the President Donald Trump's administration said on Friday it would move ahead with expanded sales of the product in certain Midwestern states. The U.S. Environmental Protection Agency said on Feb. 21 it would uphold an April 28 implementation date for a request from eight Midwest governors to allow year-round sales of gasoline containing 15% ethanol, a blend known as E15.
  • RFA Reacts to EPA Announcement on Year-Round E15. The Renewable Fuels Association (RFA) expressed gratitude for the Midwest governors' efforts to create a year-round E15 market and supported the EPA's decision to maintain the April 28, 2025, implementation date. RFA also emphasized the need for a nationwide legislative solution to allow year-round E15, citing Ohio's withdrawal of its petition as a sign of urgency.
  • Updated Information Regarding Low Carbon Fuel Standard Regulation Updates. The California Air Resources Board (CARB) announced that the Office of Administrative Law (OAL) has disapproved the proposed amendments to the Low Carbon Fuel Standard (LCFS) Regulation due to clarity issues in 26 provisions and procedural concerns. CARB plans to address these issues, release modifications for public comment, and resubmit the amendments within 120 days. Meanwhile, the current LCFS Regulation, effective since July 2020, remains in place. CARB has successfully navigated similar disapprovals before, as seen with the 2022 Commercial Harbor Craft regulation.
  • Weltec Biopower Hands Over Dairy RNG Plant in USA. In February 2025, Weltec Biopower has successfully completed the commissioning and handover of a dairy RNG (Renewable Natural Gas / biomethane) plant in Barron County, Wisconsin. After just four months of construction, the dairy farm produces 2.36 million standard cubic meters of RNG/biomethane annually (86,600 MMBTU per year).
  • Moreno Unveils New Bill To Provide Historic Investment in American Auto Workers, Freedom for Consumers. Senator Bernie Moreno (R-OH) released the Transportation Freedom Act, a new bill to provide an historic investment in American auto workers while making cars affordable for everyday Americans by eliminating government mandates that have caused vehicle prices to surge.
